labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: Document a way to commit to everything
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
Due to the way the AD is absorbed, the differences of key and nonce can be canceled out, leading to a state collision. This is why AEGIS isn't fully committing.

Something to explore is slower absorption, giving control only on half an AES word before the permutation:

AEGIS-256:

```
Absorb(ai):
    t0, t1 = Split(ai, 64)
    Update(ZeroPad(t0, 128))
    Update(ZeroPad(t1, 128))
```

AEGIS-128L:

```
Absorb(ai):
    t0, t1, t2, t3 = Split(ai, 64)
    Update(ZeroPad(t0, 128), ZeroPad(t1, 128))
    Update(ZeroPad(t2, 128), ZeroPad(t3, 128))
```

Eliminating the differences in just a single word would then require up to 2^64 input blocks, and differences would be reintroduced on the following state updates. 

Associated data is usually pretty short, so performance impact should be acceptable.

A more conservative way that doesn't require a different absorption procedure is to hash the AD, i.e. recommend using:

```
Encrypt(msg, H(ad), key, nonce)
```

instead of

```
Encrypt(msg, ad, key, nonce)
```

As long as `H` is preimage-resistant, finding associated data that cancel the key and nonce differences becomes impractical.

Sure, hashing has a cost, and the security level can degrade to the one of the hash function. But only the AD has to be hashed, it doesn't require changes to the specification nor implementations, and that can be documented as a simple recommendation for protocols that require a fully committing scheme.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body:
> Something to explore is slower absorption, giving control only on half an AES word before the permutation

Whilst I like this idea, isn't it too late to make such a change?

> A more conservative way that doesn't require a different absorption procedure is to hash the AD

This seems worth mentioning. Security Considerations section?

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
> Whilst I like this idea, isn't it too late to make such a change?

It is.

We can document this as optionally calling `Encrypt(msg, Expand(ad), key, nonce)` for protocols requiring full commitment, and define `Expand(ad)` as something like:

```
a = Split(ZeroPad(ad, 128), 64)
b = {}
for x in a:
   b = b || ZeroPad(x, 128)
return b 
```

i.e. insert a 64 bit empty block every 64 bit ad block.

But this is not ideal, and looks like a last-minute patch. Also requires a publication to refer to.

Hashing the AD is not as efficient, but boring in a good way.

> Security Considerations section?

Looks like right place for it.

issues.title: Add implementation status
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7942 (also recommended in https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-eckel-edm-find-code-02.html) suggests adding an "Implementation status" section, that will eventually be removed before the RFC is published.

Even though Datatracker links this repository for discussions, finding available implementations, including the reference ones, may not be obvious.

Maybe we should add an "Implementation status" section, that either includes a copy of the table with have in the `README.md` file here, or links to it.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
`draft-eckel-edm-find-code` mentions:

> A GitHub repository is associated with the I-D using the `github_repo` tag.

We should use that to add a link to this repository. I looked really hard, but didn't find how to add that tag on Datatracker. How to do this?

/cc @eckelcu

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body:
> Maybe we should add an "Implementation status" section, they either includes a copy of the table with have in the README.md file here, or links to it.

I think it would be better to link to the table since that will be kept up to date rather than new drafts. The amount of information this RFC is talking about would also make for a long section.

We should probably add `This note is to be removed before publishing as an RFC.` like in the `Discussion Venues` so we hopefully don't forget to remove it.

comments.author: eckelcu
comments.body:
> `draft-eckel-edm-find-code` mentions:
> 
> > A GitHub repository is associated with the I-D using the `github_repo` tag.
> 
> We should use that to add a link to this repository. I looked really hard, but didn't find how to add that tag on Datatracker. How to do this?
> 
> /cc @eckelcu

draft authors are able to add these tags for individual drafts. Once they become working group drafts, only the WG chairs can add them.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
Thank you @eckelcu !

That explains it.

issues.title: Downloadable test vectors
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
The document includes test vectors that are easy to parse.

But maybe it would be useful to also offer them as files that can be downloaded from this repository, in various formats (KAT, JSON, etc.)

Similar to the implementation table, the link would have to be removed before the RFC publication.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: That's a good idea. How would the 'verification failed' ones be separated? Would the different AEGIS-128/256X2 vs 4 test vectors go in the same file?

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
The Project Wycheproof test vectors include a "result" property (`valid`/`invalid`). At least for JSON, we can adopt their conventions.

And once file per variant is probably more convenient.

issues.title: "byte" vs "octet"
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
The document uses both "byte" and "octet".

Maybe we should use only "octet" everywhere.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: Would we change the `Byte(x)` function? I would prefer byte everywhere, but I presume octet eliminates any ambiguity. The ChaCha20-Poly1305 RFC seems to use both.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
I'm not a big fan of "octet" either. Even less as a function name. But we can't assume that both terms are interchangeable, as this is not true, even today (e.g. DSPs).

Maybe we can use `byte` everywhere, and mention somewhere that thorough this document, `byte`  refers to a 8-bit  sequence? I'm not sure how/where that could be doable, but it may be worth it, no matter what the ChaChaPoly RFC did.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body:
> Maybe we can use byte everywhere, and mention somewhere that thorough this document, byte refers to a 8-bit sequence? I'm not sure how/where that could be doable, but it may be worth it

I like that idea, but I'm not sure where that would go. Could we have a sentence at the top of `Conventions and Definitions`? Kind of like the [age spec](https://github.com/C2SP/C2SP/blob/main/age.md) but after the key words bit.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body: Fixed in #22

issues.title: Mention that different variants produce different [ciphertexts and] tags
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
It's not clear that the parallelism degree affects not only performance, but also the output.

We should mention somewhere that for a given `(key,nonce,ad,msg)`, different parallelism degrees can produce different authentication tags. 

If #14 is merged, we should extend that to the ciphertexts.

What would be the best place for that? In the security considerations?

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: Yes, that makes sense. I think it could go there. We haven't mentioned that different associated data affects the ciphertext for the standard variants either have we? Perhaps we could mention both in one paragraph.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
> We haven't mentioned that different associated data affects the ciphertext for the standard variants either have we? 

We haven't, but if we do, we should be cautious and set the preconditions for that to hold true. It can be a little bit complicated and confusing.

The AD and the message are absorbed the same way. 

Which implies that `Enc(ad=AA...ABB...B, msg=CC...CDD...D)` produces the same ciphertext as `Enc(ad=AA...A, msg=BB...BCC...CDD....D)` for `DD....D`.

issues.title: Rejecting weak states
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
As in many constructions, AEGIS has weak states. If the keys are randomly chosen as required, getting them is very unlikely. But with specifically crafted keys and inputs, weak states be reached.

As a safeguard, we can add an additional step in the decryption function, before `expected_tag = Finalize(|ad|, |msg|)`, that checks if all the AES blocks are identical, and returns an error if this is the case.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body: Scratch that. In order to be useful, it should be done after every update, which would be way too expensive.

issues.title: Mention that AEGIS must not not used as a hash function
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
AEGIS is a very good and fast MAC, so it could be tempting to use it as a replacement for a hash function.

But if the key is known, inputs generating state collisions can be easily crafted. So, we should make that clear somewhere, probably in the security considerations.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: I agree. That's a sensible place to put it.

issues.title: Require SHA512 for the variants with 256-bit security?
issues.author: jedisct1
issues.body:
It's 2am and I need to get some sleep before reading this, but @emanjon just dropped this:

https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/913.pdf

For AEGIS-256X, this is not an issue, as the IANA request hasn't been made yet.

For `TLS_AEGIS_256_SHA384`, I don't know if the suite name can be updated (that would be a breaking change for existing implementations, but this is fine as the document is still a draft), or if we should ask for a new one.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: I haven't read the full paper yet, but it does seem like that should be swapped. Guess [Sabrina Tanamal](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls-reg-review/NTUFnKVAqI832Jt-fe-HzT-xaJw/) who assigned that needs to be contacted/asked.

pulls.title: Document the AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X modes
pulls.author: jedisct1
pulls.body:
Reopening the PR here, since the repo was moved to the CFRG org.

Document the parallel modes.

In order to reduce redundancy and the document size, inputs and outputs of individual functions haven't been duplicated.

Not sure if we should (for clarity/consistency, maybe) or not.

Fixes https://github.com/jedisct1/draft-aegis-aead/issues/94

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body:
I think I'm going to have to leave reviewing this until the weekend if there's no rush. I've got quite a busy week, there's a lot to check over, and I'd like to compare it to your paper. I had a skim read of the text earlier, and it looked good aside from one or two things worth tweaking.

> In order to reduce redundancy and the document size, inputs and outputs of individual functions haven't been duplicated.
> Not sure if we should (for clarity/consistency, maybe) or not.

This is a difficult one. I think we could get away with it from a consistency perspective but perhaps not if it affects the clarity/requires lots of jumping around the document.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
From an implementation perspective, the parallel modes are likely to be implemented after the regular variants have been implemented.

So, the reader is expected to already be familiar with these functions and their parameters, that intentionally remain exactly the same.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
In the paper, `r` refers to the rate of the underlying AEGIS primitive, while in this document, `R` refers to the rate of the mode (= `r · ν` in the paper).

That can be confusing, but in implementations, only the mode rate is relevant, so that simplifies the specification quite a bit.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
Thanks a lot, Samuel! That was a lot to review.

All your comments should have been addressed.

comments.author: samuel-lucas6
comments.body: Well done Frank! Teamwork makes the dream work, but you did the hard part. I'll take a look later this week.

reviews.author: samuel-lucas6
reviews.body: Sorry there's quite a lot to go through. Various things are up for debate. Hopefully haven't missed anything.

comments.body: This could be moved in the file so all the references are in alphabetical order if you still want to do that.

Currently [some](https://cfrg.github.io/draft-irtf-cfrg-aegis-aead/draft-irtf-cfrg-aegis-aead.html#name-normative-references) references have full dates (day/month/year) and others only have the year. We should probably be consistent with one approach. I don't think day/month are necessary.
Is it worth rearranging these to put the ones from CAESAR first like in the bullet points below?

> based on AEGIS-256X

Typo.

Could add commas to break the sentence up:

> Some CPUs, such as Intel and Intel-compatible CPUs with the VAES extensions, include...

> AEGIS-128L or AEGIS-256

Could change to something like:

> multiple AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 instances respectively, with distinct initial states.

> 2 parallel operations

and

> can be raised to 4.

Do you prefer these as numbers?
Missing a full stop.

> WIth

Typo.

> V[j,i]

Should we have a space after the comma for these?

> `[0..D)`

What's the rationale for a non-square bracket at the end?
Can drop the comma.
Capitalise like the `Conventions and Definitions` section?

> AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 `Encrypt` function

I feel like we should say `respectively` or something to clarify that AEGIS-128X maps to AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256X maps to AEGIS-256.

> `Encrypt` function, but processes

Can drop the comma. This applies to all of the descriptions in each subsection (I won't keep adding a comment).

> `R` bit

Add a hyphen or rephrase to what's said in `Decrypt()`.
Worth rephrasing to `the degree of parallelism`?
See my comment on `Encrypt()`. If changing that, this will also need to be updated.

> ZeroPad(ctx)

This doesn't match the `Conventions and Definitions` section:

> ZeroPad(x, n): padding operation. Trailing zeros are concatenated to x until the total length is a multiple of n bits.
I'm finding this bit confusing. It feels like `Update(nonce_v, key_v)` at the bottom takes too large of an input, and aren't the key and nonce the same? Why can't it be `Update(nonce, key)`?
Same with the line below.

> `R` (`= 2 * 128 * D`) bits

Not sure about this. How about moving the brackets after:

> `R` bits (`2 * 128 * D`)

Better or worse?

> `M0` and `M1` are `128 * D` bits instead of 128 bits. `M0` and `M1` are split into 128-bit blocks, each of them updating a different AEGIS-128L state.

Perhaps worth writing this without repeating `M0` and `M1`.
Ok I think I get it; it's to do with the modified `Update()` function.
Can drop the comma.
There's no empty line before `else:` in the AEGIS-128L section.

> initizalizes

Typo.

> initizalizes

Typo.
Comma can be dropped.
Comma can be dropped.

> k0n0_v

and

> k1n0_v

Typos?

> ZeroPad(ctx)

Doesn't match the `Conventions and Definitions` section as mentioned for AEGIS-128X:

> ZeroPad(x, n): padding operation. Trailing zeros are concatenated to x until the total length is a multiple of n bits.

> ctx = Byte(i)

Why is this here?

> k0n0_v

and

> k1n1_v

Typos? Or not defined as said above. I get that this should be `k0 ^ n0`/`k1 ^ n1`.
Or this isn't defined like `k0_v, k1_v = {}, {}`.
`k0_v` and `k1_v` aren't in the loop.
I think the paper has a mistake where it says to do just the halves of the nonce.
See comments on AEGIS-128X with the `R` bits at once and whether to put the brackets before/after.
Not stating that `M is 128 * D bits instead of 128 bits` like in the AEGIS-128X section. Best to merge with the existing sentence if mentioning this.
Can drop the comma.

> Update(t, t)

Should be `Update(t)`.
There's no empty line here in the AEGIS-256 section.

> considerations

Capitalise.

> AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256X

Typo for AEGIS-256.
Perhaps put this on the same line as the previous sentence.

> , or

Can drop the comma in both cases.
Could just start with `In AEGIS-256X,`, but the `And` does work here.

Could be put on the same line as the previous sentence assuming that's readable.

`pseudo-code` can be written as one word without the hyphen.

> considerations

Capitalise.
Can drop the comma.

> 512 bit wide

Bits? Or add a hyphen.

> the parallelism degrees implementations are encouraged to support are `2` and `4`

This part doesn't make sense.

> The resulting algorithms SHOULD be named `AEGIS-128X2`, `AEGIS-128X4`, `AEGIS-256X2` and `AEGIS-256X4`.

Do we not want this as MUST? Or `The resulting algorithms are called...`.

There can also be a comma before the last `and` for consistency with other sections.

> implement it optimally

I'd replace `it`.
Should we capitalise the headings? They are in `IANA Considerations`.

> Implementations MAY not include the parallel AEGIS modes.

How does this sound:

> Implementations MAY choose not to include the parallel AEGIS modes.

> , respectively, AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256

I'd rephrase to:

> and requirements as AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 respectively.
Nod. The year is enough.
Sure. They were sorted by security level, but consistency is good.
Good catch
Having these as numbers helps to make the connection with `2X` and `4X`, but if it looks odd, I've nothing against `two` and `four`
Sure!
Square brackets mean inclusive, non-square brackets mean exclusive.

> Should we have a space after the comma for these?

I originally wrote them with a comma. However, in function definitions and calls, not having the comma improves readability: `f(v[I,j], k)` is more readable than `f(v[I, j], k)`.

Changed to: "The `Encrypt` function of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X is similar to, respectively, the AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 `Encrypt` function`"

Does it sound ok for you?
Changed the same way as the `Encrypt()` function.
Fixed.
Correct. The `Update()` function requires wider blocks than the non-parallel variants.

> > `R` (`= 2 * 128 * D`) bits
> 
> Not sure about this. How about moving the brackets after:
> 
> > `R` bits (`2 * 128 * D`)
> 
> Better or worse?

Moving the brackets after causes a unit mismatch. `2 * 128 * D` is a scalar, while `R bits` is a number of bits.

> > `M0` and `M1` are `128 * D` bits instead of 128 bits. `M0` and `M1` are split into 128-bit blocks, each of them updating a different AEGIS-128L state.
> 
> Perhaps worth writing this without repeating `M0` and `M1`.

Indeed.
dropped!

With the `for` loop right before, I think having the empty lines seems to improve readability.

However, this is inconsistent with other places where we use `else:` 

I added the empty line in the AEGIS-128L section as well. Let me know if you prefer to remove that empty line everywhere.
Sure!
Changed. Thank you!
Replaced it with `for optimal performance`. Does that sound better?
Capitalized!
Better indeed!
Better!
Fixed, thanks!
Dropped.
Done.
"or" dropped, and "And" removed.
Done.
Comma removed.
That's what I thought and makes sense. Perhaps we should leave it. The 2 looks odd to me compared to the 4 because it's mid-sentence, but they should obviously both be either numbers or words.

> Square brackets mean inclusive, non-square brackets mean exclusive.

Ok if that's common knowledge, that's fine. I was thinking about it like slicing arrays.

> not having the comma improves readability

Yes, that makes sense.

I think the respectively would be better after, but it's quite an awkward sentence. What do you think of these:

> The Encrypt function of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X is similar to the AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 Encrypt function respectively.

or

> The Encrypt functions of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X are similar to the AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 Encrypt functions respectively.

> Moving the brackets after causes a unit mismatch

Ok let's leave it. Do you prefer it with or without the `=` would be my other question?
I will have a look, but that sounds like a good idea.
That does.

Changed to:

> The Encrypt function of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X is similar to the AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 Encrypt function respectively

"respectively" at the end looks odd to me, but I trust you :)
The `=` doesn't seem necessary. Let's remove it.
Removed.
`k0n0_v, k1n1_v = {}, {}` added.
Fixed.
Added the fact that M is 128*D bits.
Good catch.
Done.

> they are based on, but can

Comma can be removed. Didn't mention this before.

> The `Encrypt` function of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X is similar to the AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 `Encrypt` function respectively

The second `function` almost sounds like it needs to be `functions`. I'm wondering if we should reword this to something like:

> The `Encrypt` function of AEGIS-128X and AEGIS-256X is similar to the `Encrypt` function of AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-256 respectively

Does that sound any better to you?
Please see my new comment on `Encrypt()`. If changing that, this will also need to be updated again.

I'm happy to leave the empty line. Is there an empty line in the AEGIS-256 section?


[FD] -> Not sure why I can't respond to that comment, but yes, there's also an empty line in the AEGIS-256 section.

> n0_v, n1_v = {}, {}

Think this can be removed.

> The parallelism degrees implementations are encouraged to support

Perhaps rephrase to:

> The degrees of parallelism implementations are encouraged to support

I misread it the first time because of `degrees` and `implementations` being next to each other.

> `M0` and `M1` are `128 * D` bits instead of 128 bits. They are split

This is definitely an improvement, but I noticed the wording for AEGIS-256X:

> `M` is `128 * D` bits instead of 128 bits and is split into 128-bit blocks, each of them updating a different AEGIS-256 state.

Do you want to combine the two sentences for AEGIS-128X or do you think it'll be more readable with them separate? For example:

> `M0` and `M1` are `128 * D` bits instead of 128 bits but are split into 128-bit blocks,
Removed!

That sounds indeed better.

Maybe a comma before "respectively" would be even better?
Indeed.
Combining the sentences is fine.

How about something like:

> The Encrypt function of AEGIS-128X resembles that of AEGIS-128L, and similarly, the Encrypt function of AEGIS-256X mirrors that of AEGIS-256

?

or:

> The Encrypt function of AEGIS-128X resembles that of AEGIS-128L, and likewise, the Encrypt function of AEGIS-256X is similar to that of AEGIS-256

?
Rephrased!
I like both of your suggestions. My preference would probably be the first.
Just a reminder that this will also need to be updated given your rephrasing.
Changed to the first suggestion. Also updated the `Decrypt` counterpart.
Yep!

pulls.title: Add a note on security against quantum attacks
pulls.author: jedisct1
pulls.body: https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/1306.pdf analyses the security of AEGIS-128L and other ciphers in the Q2 model (which is highly impractical), but also mentions that all the schemes are secure in the Q1 model.

So, mention this right in the first paragraph about these modes.

pulls.title: Ask IANA to register identifiers for the parallel modes
pulls.author: jedisct1

pulls.title: AEGIS-X: include the parallelism degree in the context
pulls.author: jedisct1
pulls.body:
This is a breaking change, and it is not strictly necessary, but since it is not too late, it may be desirable.

Currently, if a (key,nonce) is reused with different variants of the same security level, the key streams initialized with the same context will be the same.

For example, with AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-128X2, the 128L key stream will be the same as the first half of the 128X2 key stream.

Tags will be different, but reusing a (key,nonce) with different variants would still reveal the difference between the messages.

We can document that (key,nonce) pairs must not be reused with different algorithms.

But a better alternative would be to include the degree in the context.

We actually use (degree - 1), so that when the degree is 1, the context remains all-zero, and it doesn't break the equivalence between AEGIS-128L and AEGIS-128X1.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body: If you agree with that change, the paper will be updated as well.

comments.author: jedisct1

comments.author: jedisct1

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
The `ZeroPad` repetition was originally there because the context itself was the byte, not the padding.

But that didn't really match the way it would actually be implemented. Describing it as a mask solved it :)

pulls.title: "respectively" doesn't seem necessary here.
pulls.author: jedisct1
pulls.body: Also, the AEGIS paper is unlikely to change. We shouldn't give the feeling that it's still a moving target.

pulls.title: Say bytes instead of octets
pulls.author: samuel-lucas6
pulls.body: Let me know what you think of the sentence placement and phrasing.

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
Not a big fan of "instead of" (-> "why didn't you use 'octet', then?")

How about "interchangeably with" instead?

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body: Thank you Samuel!

pulls.title: Mention that AEGIS must not be used as a hash function
pulls.author: samuel-lucas6

comments.author: jedisct1
comments.body:
Looks good!

Thank you Samuel!
"performing encryption with the message" can be a bit confusing, as we don't encrypt anything. Maybe replace it with "by calling the `Encrypt()` function"?
"Both algorithms" -> maybe "All the variants"?
Both good suggestions. I put `Both algorithms` because that was used for the nonce-respecting setting part, which we might want to change.
Now I'm debating `Each variant`, but then `However, they` may have to change.
